name: Pak, Richard
recent_1: Pak, R., Rovira, E., & McLaughlin, A. C. (2025). Polite AI mitigates user susceptibility to AI hallucinations. Ergonomics, 68(10), 1735–1745. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2024.2434604
recent_1_synopsis: In a between-subjects experiment, participants completed a difficult quiz aided by either a polite or a neutral-toned AI chatbot that occasionally supplied hallucinated (incorrect but plausible) information. The study found that a polite conversational tone reduced users' susceptibility to acting on hallucinated output, showing that interaction style is a practical lever for calibrating trust and mitigating harm from unreliable generative AI.
recent_2: Guyton, Z., Pak, R., & Rovira, E. (2025). The role of automation etiquette and task-criticality on performance, workload, automation reliance, and user confidence. Applied Ergonomics, 125, 104430. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2024.104430
recent_2_synopsis: Using a computer-based automation task, this study manipulated automation etiquette (good vs. bad) and task-criticality in non-personified technology. Bad etiquette increased automation bias and reliance—improving performance with highly capable automation but also raising subjective workload and lowering self-confidence—demonstrating that interface politeness shapes reliance most strongly for capable automation and low-criticality tasks.
recent_3: Draheim, C., Herdener, N., Rovira, E., Melick, S. R., Coyne, J. T., Sibley, C., & Pak, R. (2025). Investigating transfer of input device practice on psychomotor performance in an aviation selection test. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251355828
recent_3_synopsis: This study examined whether practice with a particular input device transfers to psychomotor performance on a standardized aviation selection test, isolating device-specific learning from underlying ability. The findings inform fairness and validity in high-stakes personnel selection by clarifying how prior input-device experience can bias measured psychomotor performance.
seminal_1: Pak, R., Fink, N., Price, M., Bass, B., & Sturre, L. (2012). Decision support aids with anthropomorphic characteristics influence trust and performance in younger and older adults. Ergonomics, 55(9), 1059–1072. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2012.691554
seminal_1_synopsis: Using a diabetes decision-making task, this experiment showed that deliberately anthropomorphic automation altered trust, dependence, and performance differently for younger and older adults, with anthropomorphism increasing dependence on reasonably reliable aids across age groups. With roughly 180 citations (Semantic Scholar), it is one of Pak's most influential works and a foundational demonstration that surface design cues—not just reliability—shape human-automation trust, an insight central to later trust-in-automation and human-AI research.
seminal_2: Pak, R., Rogers, W. A., & Fisk, A. D. (2006). Spatial ability subfactors and their influences on a computer-based information search task. Human Factors, 48(1), 154–165. DOI: 10.1518/001872006776412180
seminal_2_synopsis: This study decomposed spatial ability into subfactors and tested their distinct contributions to performance on a web-like information search task across age groups. With roughly 58 citations (Semantic Scholar), it is foundational to Pak's program on how age-related cognitive differences predict technology use, helping establish individual-differences (spatial cognition) as a design-relevant determinant of older adults' interaction with information systems.
expertise_overview: Pak studies the psychological and cognitive factors that determine successful interaction with automated and autonomous technologies, ranging from everyday tools (smartphones, GPS, decision aids) to complex systems such as UAV control and human-robot/human-AI teaming. A central thread is trust in automation and AI—how anthropomorphism, etiquette, reliability, transparency, uncertainty communication, ethics, and trust repair shape calibrated reliance, performance, and acceptance. A second thread is cognitive aging, examining how age-related changes in attention, working memory, spatial ability, and other cognitive abilities affect older adults' ability to learn and use technology, and how displays and interfaces can be designed to support them. Methodologically he favors controlled behavioral experiments with subjective and performance measures, individual-differences and latent-variable approaches, and attention-control/working-memory constructs applied to applied human factors questions. Application domains include healthcare and medical decision support, generative AI and chatbots, aviation/personnel selection, and human-AI teaming.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. Richard Pak is a psychology professor and director of the Human Factors Institute at Clemson University (Cognition, Aging, and Technology / CAT Lab), an HFES Fellow. Confirmed via Google Scholar profile (user=Zhu3_2MAAAAJ; ~5,396 total citations, h-index 33, i10-index 67), the Clemson CAT Lab page (blogs.clemson.edu/catlab), Clemson News, and ResearchGate. The four anchor hf_papers (DOIs 10.1177/00187208251355828, 10.1177/00187208231159727, 10.1177/00187208221116952, 10.1177/00187208211032889) match this researcher's authorship and topics. All cited DOIs were verified to resolve via Crossref with matching titles/authors: recent_1 (10.1080/00140139.2024.2434604, Ergonomics, Pak/Rovira/McLaughlin), recent_2 (10.1016/j.apergo.2024.104430, Applied Ergonomics, Guyton/Pak/Rovira), recent_3 (10.1177/00187208251355828, pre-verified hf_paper), seminal_1 (10.1080/00140139.2012.691554, Ergonomics, Pak/Fink/Price/Bass/Sturre), seminal_2 (10.1518/001872006776412180, Human Factors, Pak/Rogers/Fisk). Seminal citation counts are from Semantic Scholar as of June 2026 (2012 anthropomorphic paper ~180; 2006 spatial ability paper ~58); these are lower than Google Scholar figures, which are typically higher. Total citation/h-index figures from Google Scholar (June 2026). No fabricated DOIs; no unverifiable citations included. A 2025/2026 Ergonomics paper ('GenAI as complete cognitive automation for untrained users at population scale,' Pak/Rovira/McLaughlin) was found but excluded because no resolving DOI could be confirmed at time of research.
